name: Radegast
description: Radegast, also known as Radagast, Redigast, or Riedegost, is a deity associated with hospitality, war, and possibly the sun in Slavic mythology. He was primarily venerated by the Polabian Slavs, particularly in the Rethra (also known as Radgosc) temple, a significant religious center in what is now northern Germany. Historical accounts from chroniclers like Thietmar of Merseburg describe Radegast as a fierce god, with a temple adorned with animal horns and sacred groves. He was consulted before embarking on military campaigns, and his priests played a crucial role in interpreting omens and guiding the tribes. While the exact nature of Radegast's powers and attributes remains debated due to limited and sometimes conflicting historical sources, he is often depicted as a warrior god, a protector of travelers, and a figure connected to fertility and abundance. Some interpretations also link him to the sun, suggesting a connection to light and warmth. His influence extended beyond war, encompassing aspects of community well-being and prosperity. The destruction of Rethra in the 12th century marked a decline in his worship, but Radegast remains a significant figure in the study of pre-Christian Slavic beliefs, representing the complex interplay of war, hospitality, and possibly solar symbolism within their religious system. He is often seen as a symbol of Slavic resistance to Germanic expansion and Christianization.
subtitle: God of Hospitality, War, and Possible Sun

keyMyths.title: Consultation Before War
keyMyths.description: Before embarking on military campaigns, Slavic tribes would consult Radegast's priests at Rethra. The priests would interpret omens to determine the god's will and ensure success in battle.

keyMyths.title: Temple at Rethra
keyMyths.description: The temple at Rethra was a significant religious center dedicated to Radegast. It housed idols, animal horns, and sacred groves, serving as a focal point for worship and community gatherings.

keyMyths.title: Destruction of Rethra
keyMyths.description: The destruction of Rethra in the 12th century by Christian forces marked a decline in Radegast's worship. This event significantly impacted the religious landscape of the Polabian Slavs.

sources.author: Thietmar of Merseburg
sources.description: A chronicle detailing events in the Holy Roman Empire and surrounding regions, including descriptions of Slavic customs and religious practices.
